Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.

After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Looking at the image, the player (white + green) is now right next to the left blue square. The white square appears to be inside or adjacent to the blue square's frame. Let me check the exact positions and see if the player entered the blue square.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("All nodes:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] not in ['w', 'P']:  # skip background and HUD
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, children={n['children']}")

# Check adjacency
print(f"\nAdjacency: {seg['adjacency_list']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The node IDs have shifted because the player moved. Let me re-analyze:
- Node 3: charcoal frame at row 28-31, col 16-19 - this is the LEFT blue square, and it now has child node 6 (blue inner square)
- Node 4: white (W), 4 pixels, at row 28, col 20-23 - this is the PLAYER HEAD, right next to the left blue square
- Node 7: green (N), 12 pixels, at row 29-31, col 20-23 - this is the PLAYER BODY
- The player is now at row 28-31, col 20-23, right next to the left blue square (which is at col 16-19)
Looking at the adjacency list: [3, 4] means node 3 (left blue square frame) is adjacent to node 4 (white player head). Also [3, 7] means node 3 is adjacent to node 7 (green player body). And [4, 7] means player head and body are adjacent.
The player is now positioned right next to the left blue square. Let me try pressing SPACE to see if there's an interaction.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player has navigated to the left blue square. Player head (white) at row 28, col 20-23 and body (green) at row 29-31, col 20-23. The left blue square frame is at row 28-31, col 16-19. Player is adjacent to it.
Goal model: Need to interact with the blue-framed squares. SPACE might trigger an interaction.
Plan: Try pressing SPACE while adjacent to the left blue square to see if it triggers something.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try SPACE to interact with the left blue square
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
